questions.question: Which of the following correctly states the general rule for when acceptance takes effect?

options.A: Acceptance takes effect when the offeree forms a mental intention to accept
options.B: Acceptance takes effect when it is communicated to and received by the offeror
options.C: Acceptance takes effect when the offeree posts or dispatches the acceptance
options.D: Acceptance takes effect when the offeror reads the acceptance

questions.explanation: The general rule is that acceptance must be communicated to the offeror and takes effect upon receipt: Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp [1955]. The offeree bears the risk of non-communication. The postal rule is a well-established exception to this general principle.

questions.question: The postal rule, established in Adams v Lindsell [1818], provides that:

options.A: Acceptance by post is only valid if the offeror specified that post was an acceptable means of communication
options.B: Acceptance is complete and effective from the moment the letter of acceptance is properly posted
options.C: Acceptance takes effect when the posted acceptance is received and read by the offeror
options.D: Acceptance by post is never valid for contracts involving land

questions.explanation: In Adams v Lindsell [1818], the court established that where post is the contemplated or reasonable means of communication, acceptance takes effect at the moment of posting — even if the letter is delayed, lost, or never received. This places the risk of postal delay on the offeror, who chose (or permitted) that mode of communication.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a condition for the postal rule to apply?

options.A: Post must be the contemplated or reasonable means of communication
options.B: The letter of acceptance must be properly addressed and stamped
options.C: The offeror must have already received the offer in writing
options.D: The letter must be placed in the control of the postal system

questions.explanation: The postal rule requires that post be a contemplated or reasonable mode of communication and that the letter be properly posted (correctly addressed, stamped, and placed in the postal system). There is no requirement that the offeror have previously received anything in writing — the rule applies to the communication of acceptance, not the offer.

questions.question: In Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp [1955], Denning LJ held that acceptance sent by telex (an instantaneous communication) took effect:

options.A: When the message was transmitted from the sender's machine
options.B: When the message was received and printed at the recipient's office
options.C: When the postal rule was applied by analogy to electronic communications
options.D: When the recipient acknowledged receipt of the telex

questions.explanation: In Entores, the Court of Appeal held that for instantaneous modes of communication (such as telex, telephone, and later fax and email), the postal rule does not apply. Acceptance takes effect when it is received at the offeror's location — not when sent. Denning LJ used the analogy of a person shouting across a river: if the words are drowned out, there is no communication.

questions.question: Silence cannot generally constitute acceptance because:

options.A: Silence is always ambiguous and impossible to interpret as agreement
options.B: Imposing contractual obligations on the basis of inaction would violate the offeree's freedom not to contract
options.C: The law requires all contracts to be made in writing
options.D: Silence constitutes a counter-offer in all circumstances

questions.explanation: The rule that silence cannot constitute acceptance was confirmed in Felthouse v Bindley [1862]. An offeror cannot impose a contract on an offeree by stipulating that silence will amount to acceptance. To allow this would undermine the offeree's autonomy and freedom not to contract. Acceptance requires a positive act of communication (verbal, written, or by conduct).

questions.question: In Felthouse v Bindley [1862], the uncle wrote to his nephew saying 'If I hear no more, I consider the horse mine at £30 15s.' The court held:

options.A: A contract was formed because the nephew had mentally decided to accept
options.B: No contract was formed because silence did not constitute acceptance
options.C: A contract was formed because the nephew had instructed the auctioneer not to sell the horse
options.D: No contract was formed because the price was uncertain

questions.explanation: In Felthouse v Bindley, the nephew had mentally accepted the uncle's offer and asked the auctioneer not to sell the horse, but had not communicated acceptance to the uncle. The court held that no contract existed. Acceptance must be communicated to the offeror — a mental decision to accept, unaccompanied by communication, is insufficient. The uncle could not impose a contract through silence.

questions.question: Acceptance must be unequivocal and mirror the terms of the offer. Which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: A conditional acceptance (subject to further terms) constitutes a valid acceptance
options.B: A purported acceptance which varies the terms of the offer is a counter-offer, not an acceptance
options.C: An acceptance which adds minor administrative terms is always a counter-offer
options.D: Acceptance must use exactly the same words as the offer

questions.explanation: The mirror image rule requires that acceptance must correspond exactly with the offer. A purported acceptance that introduces new or varied terms is a counter-offer, not an acceptance, and terminates the original offer (Hyde v Wrench [1840]). However, acceptance need not use the same words — any unambiguous assent to all the offer's terms suffices.

questions.question: Where an offeror prescribes a specific method of acceptance, which of the following is correct?

options.A: The offeree must comply strictly with the prescribed method or no contract is formed
options.B: The offeree may use any equally or more expeditious method, unless the offeror has insisted exclusively on the prescribed method
options.C: Any written method of acceptance will be equally valid regardless of what the offeror prescribed
options.D: A prescribed method of acceptance can only be waived by the offeree, not the offeror

questions.explanation: Where an offeror prescribes a mode of acceptance, this is generally for the offeror's benefit. An offeree may use an equally expeditious or more reliable method unless the offeror has expressly and exclusively insisted on only that method (Manchester Diocesan Council for Education v Commercial and General Investments [1970]). The test is whether a substantially equivalent method adequately protects the offeror's interests.

questions.question: Acceptance by conduct is possible in contract law. Which case best illustrates this principle?

options.A: Hyde v Wrench [1840]
options.B: Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893]
options.C: Felthouse v Bindley [1862]
options.D: Harvey v Facey [1893]

questions.explanation: Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893] is the classic illustration of acceptance by conduct. Mrs Carlill accepted the unilateral offer by performing the requested act (using the smoke ball as directed). In unilateral contracts, acceptance is complete upon full performance of the requested act; no separate communication is required.

questions.question: The postal rule applies to:

options.A: Email communications sent over the internet
options.B: Telephone conversations
options.C: Letters and telegrams sent by post where post is the contemplated means of communication
options.D: Telex and fax transmissions

questions.explanation: The postal rule, established in Adams v Lindsell [1818] and confirmed in Household Fire Insurance v Grant [1879], applies to postal communications (letters and telegrams sent via post) where post is the contemplated or reasonable means of acceptance. It does not apply to instantaneous communications — Entores v Miles Far East Corp [1955] confirmed telex is outside the rule, and the same principle applies to fax and email.

questions.question: In Household Fire Insurance Co v Grant [1879], the Court of Appeal held that a letter of acceptance which was lost in the post:

options.A: Did not form a contract because the offeror never received the acceptance
options.B: Formed a valid contract because the postal rule applied and acceptance was complete upon posting
options.C: Was invalid because the offeror had not authorised the use of post
options.D: Could only form a contract if the offeree could prove the letter had been posted

questions.explanation: In Household Fire Insurance Co v Grant [1879], the Court of Appeal held that a valid contract existed even though the letter of acceptance was lost in post and never received. Because the postal rule applied, acceptance was complete at the moment of posting. The offeror bears the risk of postal loss once post is a contemplated means of communication.

questions.question: Can an offeror revoke an offer after a postal acceptance has been sent but before the offeror receives it?

options.A: Yes, because revocation is effective upon communication to the offeree
options.B: Yes, because the contract is not formed until the offeror receives the acceptance
options.C: No, because the postal rule means the contract was formed at the moment of posting, making any subsequent revocation ineffective
options.D: Yes, but only if revocation is sent by a faster means than the original postal acceptance

questions.explanation: Under the postal rule, a contract is formed at the moment the acceptance is validly posted. Any attempt by the offeror to revoke after that moment is ineffective because the contract already exists. The offeror cannot withdraw an offer once the offeree has validly posted an acceptance, even if the acceptance has not yet arrived.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly states when acceptance of an online order takes effect under Irish law?

options.A: When the customer clicks 'submit order' on the website
options.B: When the seller's server receives and processes the order
options.C: When the seller sends an order acknowledgment email
options.D: When the seller sends a specific order confirmation (as opposed to an automated acknowledgment)

questions.explanation: Under the Electronic Commerce Act 2000 (Ireland) and the general rule for instantaneous communications, e-commerce acceptance occurs when the offeror (seller) communicates acceptance. A mere automated acknowledgment of receipt is typically not acceptance — a specific confirmation email from the seller constitutes acceptance. This reflects the Entores principle that acceptance of instantaneous communications is effective on receipt.

questions.question: Which of the following is an exception to the requirement that acceptance must be communicated?

options.A: Acceptance of an offer for the sale of land
options.B: Acceptance in the context of a unilateral contract by performance of the requested act
options.C: Acceptance made through a solicitor on behalf of the offeree
options.D: Acceptance of a written offer by telephone

questions.explanation: In a unilateral contract, acceptance occurs by performance of the requested act, with no separate communication of acceptance required. This was confirmed in Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893]. The offer is accepted when the condition is fully performed. Notification of acceptance is waived by the offeror's terms in such cases.

questions.question: The offeror may waive the requirement of communication of acceptance. What is the effect of such a waiver?

options.A: The waiver is invalid as communication is an essential requirement of contract formation
options.B: The offeree can accept merely by performing the act or fulfilling the condition without communicating to the offeror
options.C: The contract is only formed when the offeror becomes aware of the performance
options.D: The waiver must be contained in the written terms of the offer to be effective

questions.explanation: The offeror, as master of the offer, can waive the requirement of communication of acceptance. Where waived (express or implied from the terms of a unilateral offer), the offeree need only perform the requested act or condition for the contract to be formed. This principle underlies unilateral contracts such as that in Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893].

questions.question: In Dooley v Egan (1938), an Irish case, the court was asked to determine whether a contract had been formed. The case is authority for the proposition that:

options.A: Acceptance by post is not effective until received in Ireland
options.B: An acceptance must correspond exactly with the offer for a contract to be formed
options.C: Oral acceptance of a written offer is valid and binding where the terms are agreed
options.D: An acceptance sent by telegram is governed by the postal rule

questions.explanation: Irish courts have consistently applied the general rule that acceptance may be oral or by conduct provided it communicates unequivocal assent to the offer's terms. The form of the offer (written or oral) does not prescribe the form of acceptance unless the offeror expressly requires it. Oral acceptance of a written offer can form a valid contract.

questions.question: Where two parties make identical cross-offers (each unaware of the other's offer), is a contract formed?

options.A: Yes, because the offers are identical and therefore constitute mutual agreement
options.B: No, because acceptance requires knowledge of the offer being accepted, and cross-offers do not satisfy this requirement
options.C: Yes, because the courts imply acceptance in cases of simultaneous identical offers
options.D: Only if the offers are made in writing and witnessed

questions.explanation: Cross-offers do not form a contract. In Tinn v Hoffman [1873], parties simultaneously sent offers on identical terms. The court held no contract existed because neither party had accepted the other's offer — each offer was sent without knowledge of the other. Acceptance requires a conscious response to an offer known to the offeree.

questions.question: Where acceptance is by conduct and the offeror has no knowledge that the offeree has begun performing, which principle best describes the position?

options.A: No contract is formed until the offeror is notified of performance
options.B: In unilateral contracts, the contract is formed by performance of the act, not by its communication to the offeror
options.C: Performance without communication is always treated as a counter-offer
options.D: The offeree must obtain the offeror's prior consent before beginning performance

questions.explanation: In unilateral contracts, the offeror waives communication of acceptance by making the offer conditional on performance of an act. Once the act is completed, the contract is formed — regardless of whether the offeror is immediately notified (Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893]). The nature of the unilateral offer determines whether notification is required.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly states the rule where an offeree attempts to accept a revoked offer, unaware the offer has been revoked?

options.A: The purported acceptance is valid because the offeree lacked knowledge of revocation
options.B: The purported acceptance is ineffective because revocation, once communicated to the offeree, terminates the offer
options.C: The contract is voidable at the offeree's option due to the offeror's breach
options.D: The courts will enforce the acceptance on the grounds of estoppel

questions.explanation: Once an offer has been validly revoked and that revocation communicated to the offeree, the offer no longer exists and cannot be accepted. If the offeree, aware of the revocation, purports to accept, no contract is formed. However, if revocation has NOT been communicated, it is ineffective and the offer can still be accepted.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the position where an offeree posts an acceptance and then sends a faster communication (e.g., email) withdrawing it before the letter arrives?

options.A: The withdrawal is effective because it was received before the posted acceptance
options.B: The contract is formed at the moment of posting and the subsequent withdrawal is ineffective
options.C: The offeree may withdraw a posted acceptance provided withdrawal is received before the letter
options.D: Irish courts have not addressed this issue and the position is uncertain

questions.explanation: Under the strict postal rule, a contract is formed the moment acceptance is posted. A subsequent withdrawal communicated by faster means (even if received before the letter) cannot undo the already-formed contract. The position was considered (though not definitively resolved) in Countess of Dunmore v Alexander [1830] and remains the orthodox approach that posting completes acceptance irrevocably.
